data.title: Is It Time To Reconsider American Express (AXP) After The Recent Share Price Pullback
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether American Express shares still offer value after their long run, the key is to understand how the current price stacks up against a range of valuation checks.
  • The stock last closed at US$337.50, with recent returns of 9.7% over 1 year, 97.3% over 3 years and 172.2% over 5 years. Over the past week and month the share price declined by 6.0% and 7.5% respectively, and year to date the return is 9.5% lower.
  • Recent headlines around American Express have focused on its role as a major payments and card network, along with broader discussions about consumer spending trends and credit quality for large U.S. lenders. These themes help frame how investors are thinking about growth potential and risk, which often feeds directly into short term share price moves.
  • Our Simply Wall St Valuation analysis gives American Express a value score of 2 out of 6. We will unpack this using different valuation approaches, and later in the article we will look at an even more useful way to think about what this score really means for you.

American Express scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: American Express Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how effectively American Express turns shareholder capital into profits, then compares those profits to the return investors usually expect. The key idea is simple: if the company is earning more on its equity than the required return, that “excess” can support a higher intrinsic value per share.

For American Express, book value is $48.80 per share and the average return on equity is 36.58%. That translates into a stable earnings figure of $20.63 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 13 analysts. Against a cost of equity of $4.67 per share, this leaves an excess return of $15.96 per share, which is what the model treats as value created over and above the required return.

The model also uses a stable book value of $56.41 per share, sourced from weighted future Book Value estimates from 9 analysts, to anchor those excess returns over time. Putting this all together, the Excess Returns model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $383.55 per share, which is around 12.0% above the recent share price of $337.50. On this basis, the shares screen as undervalued on excess returns.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests American Express is undervalued by 12.0%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 53 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for American Express.

Approach 2: American Express Price vs Earnings

The P/E ratio is usually the cleanest quick check for profitable companies, because it links what you pay directly to the earnings that each share currently generates. It gives you a sense of how many dollars the market is willing to pay today for one dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “fair” P/E depends on what investors expect from the business and how much risk they see. Higher expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while lower growth expectations or higher risk tend to support a lower P/E.

American Express currently trades on a P/E of 21.66x. That is above the Consumer Finance industry average of 8.05x and close to the peer group average of 21.91x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for American Express is 20.49x. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what a reasonable P/E could be, given factors like the company’s earnings profile, industry, margins, size and risk characteristics.

Because the Fair Ratio folds in these company specific drivers, it can be more useful than a simple comparison with peers or the industry as a whole. With the current P/E of 21.66x sitting above the Fair Ratio of 20.49x, the shares screen as slightly expensive on this metric.

Result: OVERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 23 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your American Express Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. On Simply Wall St’s Community page you can use Narratives, where you set out your own story for American Express, plug in assumptions for future revenue, earnings, margins and a fair value, see that story turned into a full forecast and valuation that updates automatically when new results or news arrive, then compare your Fair Value with the current price. You can also compare it against very different community views, such as a more cautious American Express narrative with a Fair Value of about US$284 and a more optimistic one closer to US$462. This allows you to decide for yourself whether the current market price looks high, low or roughly in line with the story you believe.

For American Express however we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading American Express Narratives:

🐂 American Express Bull Case

Fair value in this bullish narrative: US$354.83 per share.

Implied discount to this fair value versus the recent price of US$337.50: about 4.9% undervalued using ((354.83 - 337.50) / 354.83).

Revenue growth assumption in this storyline: 10.28% a year.

  • Focus is on premium cardmembers, younger demographics and international expansion, with an emphasis on stable margins and long run earnings resilience.
  • Analysts in this camp build their view around long term revenue growth near 10.6% a year, steady profit margins around the mid teens and ongoing share buybacks reducing the share count.
  • Key risks they highlight include competition in premium cards, shifts toward alternative payments and the cost of keeping rewards, engagement and technology competitive.

🐻 American Express Bear Case

Fair value in this more cautious narrative: US$308.19 per share.

Implied premium to this fair value versus the recent price of US$337.50: about 9.5% overvalued using ((337.50 - 308.19) / 308.19).

Revenue growth assumption in this storyline: 10.81% a year.

  • This view accepts solid revenue and earnings projections but argues that the current share price already reflects strong product refreshes, membership model changes and acquisition benefits.
  • The narrative leans on recent revenue and EPS estimates tied to new card growth, dining and digital acquisitions and ongoing refreshes across major co branded and consumer cards.
  • Supporters of this case point to factors such as strong past stock performance, rich card fee revenue and some international challenges as reasons to be careful about how much you pay for that growth.

Kuroda Group (TSE:287A) has put up a mixed set of Q3 2026 numbers, with Q2 revenue at ¥30.4b and basic EPS of ¥24.73, alongside trailing twelve month EPS growth of 29.1% even as revenue eased 2.9% and net profit margin moved from 1.7% to 2.3%. Over recent quarters the company has seen quarterly revenue range from ¥29.6b to ¥31.3b and basic EPS move between ¥10.39 and ¥27.61, while trailing twelve month EPS shifted from ¥55.77 to ¥65.39 as total revenue moved from ¥139.3b to ¥120.4b. For investors, this combination of softer top line and better margins sets up a results season focused firmly on how durable the profitability picture really is.

See our full analysis for Kuroda Group.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to compare them with the most commonly held narratives around Kuroda Group to see which views the latest margins support and which ones the results start to challenge.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Margins Improve Despite Softer Sales

  • Over the last 12 months, revenue eased 2.9% while earnings rose 29.1%. This lifted the net profit margin from 1.7% to 2.3% even though the top line softened.
  • What is interesting for a more bullish take is that this higher 2.3% margin is coming alongside quarterly revenue drifting in a tight band of about ¥29.6b to ¥31.3b, which suggests:
    • Supporters of the bullish view can point to EPS on a trailing basis moving from ¥55.77 to ¥65.39 while total revenue moved from ¥139.3b to ¥120.4b. Profitability per share has been higher even as sales have been lower.
    • On the other hand, cautious investors may flag that earnings growth is currently tied to this margin lift during a 2.9% revenue decline, so they will watch closely how that margin behaves if sales pressure continues.

Quarterly EPS Swings Around ¥10 to ¥27

  • Across the recent quarters in the data, basic EPS has moved between roughly ¥10.39 and ¥27.61 while net income for individual quarters ranged from ¥441 million to ¥1,172 million. This shows that profitability per share can vary quite a bit even when quarterly revenue sits around ¥29b to ¥31b.
  • Critics who lean more bearish often focus on this kind of variability, and the numbers here give them both support and pushback:
    • Bears can point out that trailing twelve month EPS has moved from ¥92.20 to ¥65.39 while trailing twelve month net income went from ¥3,914 million to ¥2,776 million, so the most recent 12 month snapshot is lower than an earlier period in the dataset.
    • At the same time, the same dataset also shows earnings 29.1% higher year on year alongside the margin shift from 1.7% to 2.3%, so the latest year still looks stronger than the year before even if it sits below the earlier ¥92.20 EPS peak.

P/E Of 15.7x And DCF Gap

  • The shares are trading at ¥1,029 with a trailing P/E of 15.7x, compared with 16x for the JP Electronic industry average and 20.8x for peers. The provided DCF fair value is ¥2,504.27, which is about 59% higher than the current share price.
  • Supporters of a more optimistic stance see these valuation figures as important context for the recent earnings profile:
    • Bulls would highlight that the 15.7x P/E sits below both the industry and peer averages even after a 29.1% earnings rise and margin improvement to 2.3%. They view this as a potential disconnect between price and recent profitability.
    • They may also point to the wide gap between the ¥1,029 market price and the ¥2,504.27 DCF fair value, arguing that if earnings and margins stay around current levels, this discount leaves room for sentiment to adjust toward the DCF figure.

Bulls argue that a lower P/E and the wide DCF gap could signal a pricing mismatch for a company that has grown earnings faster than revenue, and if you want to see how that story is built out in full you can check the broader narrative here: 📊 Read the full Kuroda Group Consensus Narrative.

data.title: Greg Abel Backs Kraft Heinz Shift And Signals Berkshire’s Ownership Approach
data.description:
• Greg Abel, CEO of Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.B), publicly backed Kraft Heinz’s decision to cancel its previously planned separation.
  • Abel described the shift as a positive turnaround for Kraft Heinz and reaffirmed Berkshire’s commitment to the relationship.
  • The comments highlighted closer alignment between Berkshire and one of its largest consumer holdings, with potential implications for how Berkshire interacts with other portfolio companies.

Berkshire Hathaway sits at the center of several key sectors through its mix of operating businesses and large equity holdings, and Kraft Heinz is one of the most visible consumer brands in that group. Food and beverage companies have been reevaluating product portfolios, cost structures, and brand focus, and Kraft Heinz’s scrapped separation plan fits into that broader rethink. For investors watching NYSE:BRK.B, Abel’s endorsement adds a fresh data point on how Berkshire views long term partnerships with consumer staples holdings.

Abel’s comments also hint at how actively Berkshire’s leadership may weigh in when a core holding considers major structural change. For you as a shareholder or potential investor, this episode can help frame questions around Berkshire’s role as an influential owner and how that might shape decisions at other companies in its portfolio over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Berkshire Hathaway by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Berkshire Hathaway.

1 thing going right for Berkshire Hathaway that this headline doesn't cover.

For Berkshire Hathaway, Greg Abel’s support for Kraft Heinz’s reversal looks less like a one off comment and more like a signal about how the new CEO wants to run long term capital allocation. By backing management’s decision to keep the group intact, Abel is effectively prioritising operational execution and brand strength at a core consumer holding over transaction driven restructuring. That lines up with Berkshire’s long running preference for patience and limited portfolio churn, where management teams are expected to compound value rather than constantly reconfigure assets.

Advertisement

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Concentration in a large consumer holding like Kraft Heinz means execution missteps at the portfolio company level can feed through to Berkshire’s look through earnings and perceived quality of its consumer exposure.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged 2 key risks for Berkshire, including pressure on earnings expectations and a recent decline in profit margins, which can limit flexibility if portfolio companies underperform at the same time.
  • 🎁 Abel’s stance underlines Berkshire’s role as a supportive long term owner, which can help portfolio companies focus on customer competitiveness rather than short term financial engineering.
  • 🎁 A more stable relationship with Kraft Heinz can reduce the chance of forced selling or reactive moves around this stake, giving Berkshire more room to apply its patient, low turnover investment style.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, you may want to watch how Kraft Heinz executes under its unified structure, and whether Berkshire’s management becomes more vocal with other large holdings when big structural moves are on the table. Any future comments from Abel on portfolio companies, as well as updates on Berkshire’s segment level profitability and capital allocation, can give you additional context for how this approach is playing out across the wider group.

data.title: Is Goldman Sachs Group (GS) Pricing Still Justified After Strong Multi Year Share Gains
data.description:

  • The stock last closed at US$905.14, with returns of 39.8% over 1 year, 164.8% over 3 years and 223.0% over 5 years. The past month and year to date show declines of 5.9% and 1.0%, and there has been a 2.5% decline over the last week.
  • Recent market attention around large US financial institutions and ongoing discussion of capital requirements and industry regulation has kept Goldman Sachs Group in focus. These themes help frame how investors think about risk, growth potential and what they are willing to pay for bank and investment banking names.

Goldman Sachs Group delivered 39.8% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Capital Markets industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Goldman Sachs Group Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit Goldman Sachs Group can generate above the return that equity investors are assumed to require, then capitalizes that surplus to estimate what the shares might be worth today.

For Goldman Sachs Group, analysts estimate a stable earnings figure of $67.32 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity forecasts from 14 analysts. The cost of equity is put at $49.61 per share, which implies an excess return of $17.72 per share. In other words, the model assumes earnings are comfortably above the required return that shareholders expect.

The model also uses a current book value of $357.46 per share and a stable book value estimate of $402.33 per share, again based on analyst inputs, together with an average Return on Equity of 16.73%. These inputs are combined to produce an intrinsic value estimate of about $600.95 per share.

Compared with the recent share price of $905.14, this Excess Returns valuation implies the stock is about 50.6% overvalued.

Result: OVERVALUED

data.description:

Eutelsat Communications (ENXTPA:ETL) H1 2026 Results: Losses Persist as Revenue Holds Just Below Prior Levels

Eutelsat Communications (ENXTPA:ETL) has reported H1 2026 results with total revenue of €637.5 million and a basic EPS loss of €0.44, alongside trailing twelve month revenue of €1.23 billion and a basic EPS loss of €0.85. The company has seen revenue move from €640.4 million in H2 2024 to €606.2 million in H1 2025 and then to €637.5 million in H2 2025, while basic EPS has stayed in loss territory over the same sequence, ranging from a €0.25 loss per share in H2 2024 to a €1.84 loss in H1 2025 and a €0.44 loss in H2 2025. This keeps the focus firmly on earnings quality and margin pressure for investors. With the shares trading around €2.24 and losses still weighing on the income statement, the key question now is how much operating leverage and margin repair the business can realistically deliver over time.

See our full analysis for Eutelsat Communications.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set these results against the prevailing market and community narratives to see which views on Eutelsat hold up and which might need a rethink.

See what the community is saying about Eutelsat Communications

Losses Narrow To €209 Million On Similar Revenue

  • Net income loss for H2 2025 came in at €208.7 million on €637.5 million of revenue, compared with a €873.2 million loss on €606.2 million of revenue in H1 2025, while trailing 12 month losses sit at €445.2 million on €1.23b of revenue.
  • Bulls point out that LEO and government connectivity are intended to support margin recovery over time. However, the trailing 12 month loss of €445.2 million still sits against revenue of €1.23b, which:
    • Heavily contrasts with the bullish idea of future EBITDA margins of at least 60%, because current earnings are clearly far from that kind of profitability.
    • Suggests any improvement case rests on a shift in mix toward LEO and government contracts, while today’s reported figures are still dominated by loss making operations.

Big swings in profit and loss like this can make it hard to judge the longer term story just from one half year set of numbers. This is why bulls keep pointing back to the LEO roll out and contract pipeline as the core of their argument. 🐂 Eutelsat Communications Bull Case

Price To Sales Premium With Deep DCF Discount

  • At a share price of €2.24 and trailing 12 month revenue of €1.23b, Eutelsat trades on a P/S of about 0.9x, compared with peers at 0.6x and the French Media industry at 0.5x, even though the supplied DCF fair value of €9.24 sits very far above the current price.
  • Bears argue that paying a premium P/S multiple for an unprofitable company is risky, and the data gives them some support, even with the low price against DCF:
    • Losses over the past five years have grown at roughly 67.7% per year and the company is expected to remain loss making over the next three years, which does not obviously line up with a higher multiple than peers.
    • The same dataset flags elevated share price volatility over the last three months, so anyone focusing on that 0.9x P/S versus lower peer multiples may see the DCF fair value gap as less convincing without clearer progress toward profitability.

For you as a shareholder or potential investor, the tension here is that the model based DCF fair value of €9.24 suggests a large upside gap, while the premium P/S and continuing losses give bears plenty to point at. 🐻 Eutelsat Communications Bear Case

Revenue Growth Forecast Trails French Market

  • The current dataset shows Eutelsat’s revenue forecast at about 4.6% annual growth over the next few years, compared with a 5.7% forecast for the wider French market, while EPS is expected to stay negative over that period.
  • Consensus style views often lean on LEO projects, government contracts and reduced GEO CapEx to support the long term case, and the numbers partly align but also leave questions:
    • Forecast revenue growth below the broader French market, despite all the LEO investment, means the growth profile is not especially fast compared with the benchmark that many investors use.
    • The fact that forecasts still show negative EPS over the next three years sits awkwardly with analyst targets that assume Eutelsat eventually reaches profit margins similar to the GB Media industry.

data.title: Riot Platforms Texas Tax Deal And AMD Lease Reframe Growth Story
data.description:
• Riot Platforms secured approval for a 10 year property tax abatement tied to its new Texas data center.
  • The company named Advanced Micro Devices as the first tenant at the facility, signaling early commercial traction.
  • The project is intended to support both data infrastructure services and institutional scale Bitcoin mining activity.

Riot Platforms, traded as NasdaqCM:RIOT, is pushing further into large scale infrastructure with this Texas build out while still tying the project to its core Bitcoin mining focus. Shares recently closed at $15.22, with a 7.5% return year to date and a 24.0% return over the past year, alongside a 131.0% return over three years and a 78.7% decline over five years. The mix of strong multi year gains and a weaker five year result highlights how volatile the stock has been for longer term holders.

The 10 year tax abatement and AMD tenancy provide a clearer view of how Riot is working to add another leg to its business beyond pure mining operations. For investors tracking NasdaqCM:RIOT, the key questions from here involve tenant demand, how the data center is utilized over time, and how this effort affects the balance between infrastructure revenue and Bitcoin mining exposure.

The 10 year property tax abatement could lower operating costs at the new site, which may help the economics of both data infrastructure services and mining. Landing AMD as the first tenant also gives Riot an anchor customer as it scales from 25 MW toward the planned 200 MW capacity, which can matter when competing with data center operators and miners such as Marathon Digital, CleanSpark, or larger cloud focused players.

  • The long term commitment to a large Texas build out reinforces exposure to regional regulatory and energy market risks highlighted in the narrative, especially if grid conditions or legislation change.
  • The tax abatement itself is not fully captured in the broader story of power utilization and could influence future margin profiles at this site depending on how quickly capacity is filled.

  • 🎁 Revenue is forecast to grow at a double digit rate each year, and the AMD agreement supports the idea that data center customers can be part of that growth rather than relying only on mining.
  • 🎁 Analysts have also highlighted very large earnings growth in the recent past, which, together with a 10 year tax abatement and power heavy assets, underpins interest in Riot as it competes for AI and Bitcoin related demand.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, you may want to watch how quickly Riot signs additional tenants beyond AMD, the pace of build out from 25 MW toward the planned 200 MW, and any updates on institutional scale Bitcoin mining capacity at the Texas site.
